content: Gold standard: synovial fluid analysis showing needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy. ACR/EULAR classification criteria (2015) use scoring system: clinical pattern (joint involvement, flare characteristics), time course, clinical signs (erythema, tophi), serum urate level, imaging findings. Serum urate often normal during acute flare (urate shifts into joints) - recheck after flare resolves. Imaging: X-ray shows punched-out erosions with overhanging edges in chronic gout; ultrasound shows 'double contour sign' (urate on cartilage surface), tophi; dual-energy CT specifically identifies urate deposits. Differential diagnosis: septic arthritis (always consider, especially monoarticular), pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate crystals - positively birefringent, rhomboid), cellulitis, trauma. Evaluate for comorbidities: renal function, cardiovascular risk factors, metabolic syndrome components.
